past_medical_history: Coronary artery disease, status post percutaneous coronary intervention with drug-eluting stent to LAD (2014)
Hypertension, well controlled on current regimen
HSV-1 encephalitis treated with 21 days of IV acyclovir (April 2017), complicated by severe cognitive and language deficits requiring inpatient rehabilitation
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled, HbA1c 6.2% (3 months ago)
allergies: Penicillin (rash as a child, tolerates cephalosporins)
family_history: Father: deceased at 72 from myocardial infarction
Mother: Alzheimer disease, diagnosed at age 78
No family history of autoimmune disease or epilepsy

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in suburban home; wife provides assistance with IADLs since April hospitalization

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but agitated and intermittently combative. Oriented to self only; disoriented to place, time, and situation. Unable to follow multi-step commands. Speech is fluent but with frequent paraphasic errors and tangential content. Montreal Cognitive Assessment attempted but patient unable to cooperate. Exhibits paranoid ideation, stating staff are trying to poison him. Intermittent periods of catatonic-like staring lasting 10-20 seconds.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Mild right lower facial droop (residual from prior encephalitis). Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline. Intermittent orofacial dyskinesias with lip-smacking and jaw movements noted during examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone increased in bilateral upper extremities with intermittent cogwheel-like rigidity. Strength 4+/5 in right upper and lower extremities (baseline from prior illness), 5/5 on left. No pronator drift on left. Right-sided drift present (known residual). Intermittent choreiform movements of bilateral hands noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to noxious stimuli in all extremities. Detailed sensory examination limited by patient cooperation. Light touch appears grossly intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (mildly brisk). Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to reliably assess finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin due to agitation and inability to follow commands.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess safely due to agitation and combativeness. Per nursing, was ambulatory with unsteady gait prior to acute behavioral decompensation.
neurological_exam.additional: Autonomic instability noted: episodic tachycardia to 120s with blood pressure fluctuations (systolic ranging 100-170 mmHg over the past 6 hours). Two brief episodes of diaphoresis. No meningismus. Kernig and Brudzinski signs negative.

patient.chief_complaint: Confusion and aggressive behavior, 2 months after hospitalization for herpes encephalitis
patient.history_present_illness: This 63-year-old right-handed man with a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and HSV-1 encephalitis treated in April 2017 presents to the emergency department in June 2017 with worsening confusion and new-onset aggressive, erratic behavior over the past five days. His wife reports that following his discharge from inpatient rehabilitation in May, he had persistent mild cognitive deficits and word-finding difficulty, which were attributed to residual effects of his encephalitis. He was making slow but steady progress with outpatient speech therapy and occupational therapy. Approximately one week ago, his wife noticed he became increasingly irritable and suspicious, accusing her of hiding his belongings and conspiring against him. Over the ensuing days, he developed insomnia, refusing to sleep and pacing the house at night. He began speaking incoherently, mixing up words and making statements that did not make contextual sense. Two days prior to presentation, he became physically aggressive, pushing his wife when she attempted to give him his medications, and was found wandering outside the house in his pajamas at 3 AM. On the day of presentation, he had a witnessed episode of rhythmic jerking of the right arm lasting approximately 30 seconds, followed by staring and unresponsiveness for several minutes. Emergency medical services were called and he was brought to the ED. The wife is particularly concerned because his current presentation feels different from his initial encephalitis episode two months ago, which was characterized primarily by somnolence and confusion rather than aggression and paranoia. She denies any recent fevers, headache complaints, neck stiffness, or new medication changes. She notes he has been compliant with his levetiracetam and other home medications. There have been no recent sick contacts, travel, or known toxic exposures.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized, 6-7 Hz theta range activity replacing normal alpha rhythm
background.anterior_activity: Diffuse low-to-medium amplitude theta and delta activity
background.symmetry: Mildly asymmetric with greater slowing over right hemisphere, consistent with known prior injury
background.state_changes: No clear sleep architecture identified during 40-minute recording

findings.location: Bilateral hemispheres, more prominent over right frontotemporal region

findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, monomorphic delta bursts lasting 2-5 seconds
Low amplitude, poorly formed sharp transients

artifacts.impact: Intermittently obscures temporal activity during periods of agitation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient non-cooperation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating diffuse cerebral dysfunction with right hemispheric predominance. FIRDA is present, a non-specific finding. Rare right temporal sharp waves are noted, possibly related to prior structural injury. No extreme delta brush pattern. No definite electrographic seizures captured during this recording. The overall pattern is non-specific and could be seen in metabolic encephalopathy, post-infectious changes, or recurrent CNS pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 40 minutes with frequent movement and muscle artifact due to patient agitation. Continuous EEG monitoring recommended.

findings.location: Right frontotemporal lobes, including right insular cortex and right mesial temporal structures
findings.size: Approximately 4.2 x 3.1 cm area of volume loss

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Mixed signal with central hypointensity (CSF cleft) and peripheral hyperintense gliotic rim

findings.mass_effect: Ex-vacuo dilation of the right temporal horn

findings.location: Left frontal lobe, superior and middle frontal gyri
Bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter

additional_observations: No pathological contrast enhancement to suggest active inflammation, recurrent infection, or neoplasm
Ventricles symmetric aside from ex-vacuo right temporal horn dilation; no hydrocephalus
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
mri.impression: Stable chronic changes from prior HSV-1 encephalitis with encephalomalacia in the right frontotemporal region and residual gliosis in the left frontal lobe. No evidence of recurrent acute encephalitis on current imaging. No restricted diffusion, no pathological enhancement, and no new lesions compared to prior studies. Chronic small vessel ischemic disease as expected for age and cardiovascular risk factors. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Non-specific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads (V5-V6, I, aVL) — likely secondary to LVH
No acute ST elevation or depression suggestive of ischemia
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia with left ventricular hypertrophy and secondary repolarization changes. No acute ischemic changes. The tachycardia may be related to agitation, pain, or autonomic dysregulation. Borderline prolonged QTc warrants monitoring, particularly if QT-prolonging medications are administered.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL (L), Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), Glucose 142 mg/dL (H), CRP 1.8 mg/dL (H), ESR 22 mm/hr (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal_bands: Present (4 bands, not matched in serum) — significance uncertain

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cm. WBC: 8 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 52 mg/dL (reference: 15-45). Glucose: 62 mg/dL (reference: 40-70) (ratio 0.44 (serum glucose 142; reference CSF/serum ratio: 0.6-0.8)). HSV_1_PCR: Not detected. HSV_2_PCR: Not detected. VZV_PCR: Not detected. Enterovirus_PCR: Not detected. Gram_stain: No organisms seen. Cytology: No malignant cells identified. Oligoclonal_bands: Present (4 bands, not matched in serum) — significance uncertain. IgG_index: 0.78 (reference: 0.28-0.66) — mildly elevated.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDAR_antibody_CSF Positive (titer 1:32)  (H), Anti-NMDAR_antibody_serum Positive (titer 1:10)  (H).

additional_observations: Lungs: Clear, no masses or nodules. No lymphadenopathy.
Mediastinum: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.
Liver: Normal size and attenuation, no focal lesions.
Kidneys: Bilateral simple renal cysts (largest 1.8 cm right kidney). No hydronephrosis.
No testicular masses identified (though ultrasound is superior for testicular evaluation).
Bones: Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. No lytic or blastic lesions.
output.impression: No evidence of occult malignancy on CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis. No lymphadenopathy or suspicious masses. In a 63-year-old male with anti-NMDAR encephalitis, paraneoplastic association is less common than in young females with ovarian teratoma, but surveillance imaging is still appropriate. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 17 cm. WBC: 12 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 58 mg/dL (reference: 15-45). Glucose: 58 mg/dL (reference: 40-70) (ratio 0.48 (serum glucose 121)). HSV_1_PCR: Not detected (repeat confirmation). Anti-NMDAR_antibody_CSF: Positive (titer 1:32). Oligoclonal_bands: Present (5 bands, unmatched in serum). IgG_index: 0.82 (elevated). Cytology: No malignant cells.

Imaging_Result.value: Not applicable — patient is male. Consider testicular ultrasound or CT abdomen/pelvis for paraneoplastic workup in male patients.

output.query: post-herpes simplex virus autoimmune encephalitis anti-NMDAR antibodies

results.title: Post-herpes simplex encephalitis autoimmune encephalitis: a distinct entity with unique clinical, immunological, and treatment features
results.key_finding: 20-27% of HSV encephalitis patients develop post-HSV autoimmune encephalitis, most commonly mediated by anti-NMDAR antibodies. Distinct from classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis: older age, male predominance, choreoathetosis as presenting feature.

results.title: Frequency, symptoms, risk factors, and outcomes of autoimmune encephalitis after herpes simplex encephalitis
results.key_finding: Autoimmune encephalitis developed in 27% of HSV encephalitis survivors at a median of 42 days post-infection. Anti-NMDAR antibodies were the most common (64%), followed by anti-unknown neuronal surface antibodies.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis after herpes simplex virus encephalitis: an emerging disease with distinct clinical and paraclinical features
results.key_finding: First systematic description of post-HSV anti-NMDAR encephalitis. MRI may show residual HSV changes rather than typical anti-NMDAR patterns. CSF may show lower antibody titers than classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

output.summary: Post-herpes simplex virus autoimmune encephalitis is an increasingly recognized complication occurring in 20-27% of HSV encephalitis survivors, typically 2-8 weeks after the initial infection. Anti-NMDAR antibodies are the most common, though antibodies to other neuronal surface antigens also occur. Unlike classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis (which affects predominantly young women and is associated with ovarian teratoma), post-HSV cases affect a broader demographic including older males. Choreoathetosis, behavioral changes, and seizures are prominent features. MRI often shows residual HSV changes rather than the mesial temporal FLAIR signal typical of classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis. Treatment includes immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone, IVIG, plasma exchange) with escalation to rituximab if needed. Tumor association is rare in post-HSV cases.

output.proposed: IV methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 5 days, followed by IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days
interactions: Methylprednisolone + Aspirin: Increased risk of GI bleeding. Monitor closely. Consider PPI prophylaxis.
Methylprednisolone + Lisinopril: Corticosteroids may reduce antihypertensive effect; monitor blood pressure closely.
Methylprednisolone: May worsen hyperglycemia in patient with diabetes; requires insulin sliding scale coverage.
IVIG: Risk of thromboembolic events; relevant given patient's CAD history. Ensure adequate hydration.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications to proposed regimen
warnings: High-dose corticosteroids may cause significant hyperglycemia — implement glucose monitoring protocol (every 4-6 hours)
IVIG carries risk of renal toxicity — monitor creatinine and urine output, maintain hydration
IVIG carries risk of thrombotic events — particularly relevant given coronary artery disease history; consider low-dose aspirin continuation and adequate hydration
Monitor for aseptic meningitis (paradoxical IVIG side effect)
output.formulary_status: Both medications available on hospital formulary. IVIG requires pharmacy authorization.
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) as alternative to IVIG for first-line immunotherapy
If refractory to first-line: Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x4
If refractory: Cyclophosphamide (second-line, reserve for treatment failures)

background.symmetry: Right hemispheric predominance of slowing persists
background.state_changes: Poorly differentiated sleep-wake cycles; fragments of stage II sleep noted (vertex waves, sleep spindles)

findings.morphology: Rhythmic, semi-monomorphic delta with intermittent superimposed low-amplitude beta activity
findings.state: Intermittent, occurring in runs lasting 10-30 seconds

findings.frequency: Evolving from 2 Hz rhythmic delta to 6-8 Hz theta
findings.state: Two subclinical seizures captured, duration 45-90 seconds each

findings.frequency: Delta activity at 1-2 Hz with superimposed 20-25 Hz beta brushes
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta with riding fast activity
findings.state: Intermittent, more prominent during periods of decreased responsiveness

output.impression: Continuous EEG monitoring over 48 hours reveals worsening diffuse encephalopathy with emergence of extreme delta brush-like pattern and two subclinical electrographic seizures originating from the right temporal region. Background remains diffusely slow with right hemispheric predominance. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Frequent movement artifact during agitation episodes limits interpretation in some segments.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, post-herpes simplex virus (post-HSV autoimmune encephalitis)

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV PCR negative on repeat LP; clinical presentation differs from initial episode (behavioral/psychiatric predominance vs. somnolence); no new enhancing lesions on MRI
Would not typically present with new progressive psychiatric symptoms and movement disorder weeks after clinical improvement; would not have CSF oligoclonal bands or elevated IgG index
CSF Gram stain negative, low WBC, normal glucose, low procalcitonin; no fever; presentation subacute rather than hyperacute
Extended autoimmune panel negative; CT body negative for malignancy; post-HSV context makes NMDAR most likely

differential.diagnosis: Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis)
differential.key_distinguishing: Normal TSH, normal free T4, normal anti-TPO antibodies; no thyroid disease history
No multifocal strokes on MRI; no vessel irregularities; CRP only mildly elevated; clinical course not typical
Subacute onset with psychiatric features, movement disorder, and seizures in context of recent HSV encephalitis argues against primary neurodegeneration; no characteristic MRI findings of CJD

optimal_actions.action: Perform brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for recurrent encephalitis, new lesions, or alternative pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Chronic post-HSV changes without new acute abnormalities; no enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for seizures and encephalopathy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing with FIRDA; no extreme delta brush on initial routine EEG

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with comprehensive CSF studies including HSV PCR, cell count, protein, glucose, and cytology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild pleocytosis, elevated protein, negative HSV PCR, oligoclonal bands present

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, thyroid function, ammonia, toxicology screens, HIV, RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia (SIADH), normal thyroid function, negative toxicology and infectious screens

optimal_actions.action: Order anti-NMDA receptor antibodies in CSF and serum given post-HSV context and clinical features
Obtain ECG to evaluate cardiac status and rule out cardiac contribution to tachycardia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sinus tachycardia, LVH, no acute ischemic changes

optimal_actions.action: Order CT body or PET/CT for paraneoplastic malignancy screening
Search literature on post-HSV autoimmune encephalitis to guide management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting immunotherapy for post-HSV anti-NMDAR encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for immunotherapy (methylprednisolone, IVIG) with patient's current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Steroid-diabetes interaction, IVIG thrombosis risk with CAD, GI bleeding risk with aspirin

optimal_actions.action: Initiate first-line immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone and/or IVIG

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture with HSV PCR to exclude HSV reactivation before attributing symptoms to autoimmunity
Order anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (CSF preferred) — this is the confirmatory diagnostic test
Initiate empiric antiviral therapy (acyclovir) until HSV PCR results are available
Order continuous EEG monitoring given seizure history and altered mental status
Initiate immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone, IVIG, or plasma exchange) once anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed
Screen for underlying malignancy with body imaging (CT or PET/CT)
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to psychiatric illness and initiating high-dose antipsychotics without organic workup — antipsychotics can worsen anti-NMDAR encephalitis (dyskinesias, rigidity, autonomic instability)
Discontinuing antiviral therapy before HSV PCR results return
Administering high-dose corticosteroids before excluding active HSV infection (could worsen viral encephalitis)
Performing brain biopsy when clinical presentation and antibody testing can establish the diagnosis non-invasively
Delaying immunotherapy once diagnosis is confirmed — delayed treatment is associated with worse neurological outcomes
key_reasoning_points: Temporal relationship is critical: behavioral decompensation occurring 6-8 weeks after HSV encephalitis should raise immediate suspicion for post-HSV autoimmune encephalitis, which occurs in 20-27% of HSV encephalitis survivors

The clinical presentation has shifted qualitatively — initial HSV episode presented with somnolence and confusion, while current relapse features psychiatric symptoms (paranoia, aggression), movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias, choreiform movements), and autonomic instability, which is characteristic of anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Negative HSV PCR on repeat lumbar puncture effectively excludes viral reactivation and redirects the differential toward autoimmune or post-infectious etiologies

Initial CSF is only mildly abnormal (8 WBC, protein 52) which could mislead toward a benign post-infectious course — the oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index are subtle clues to ongoing intrathecal immune activation
MRI showing only chronic post-HSV changes without new lesions is actually expected in post-HSV anti-NMDAR encephalitis — normal or stable MRI does not exclude autoimmune encephalitis
The absence of extreme delta brush on initial routine EEG is a potential pitfall — this pattern may only emerge on continuous monitoring and is seen in only 30% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases

Post-HSV anti-NMDAR encephalitis has a different demographic profile than classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis — it affects older patients and males, and is rarely associated with tumors, making this 63-year-old male presentation consistent
The hyponatremia (Na 131) may reflect SIADH, which is seen in both HSV encephalitis and anti-NMDAR encephalitis, and should not be dismissed as the sole explanation for altered mental status
Mother's Alzheimer diagnosis and patient's age might create anchoring bias toward neurodegenerative disease, but the acute-subacute timeline and post-infectious context argue against this

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: presentation occurs in an atypical demographic (older male) following confirmed HSV encephalitis, mimicking HSV reactivation or post-infectious sequelae. Initial MRI shows only chronic changes, routine EEG shows non-specific slowing, and CSF is only mildly abnormal. The critical diagnostic clue (anti-NMDAR antibodies) must be specifically requested as a follow-up test. Multiple red herrings include the mother's Alzheimer history, cardiovascular comorbidities, and the temptation to attribute symptoms to residual HSV damage.
